metadata.title: What a Review Has to Do That a Reaction Never Does
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Writing Guide candidate 06. Source chapter: writing-guide/chapters/09-evaluation-or-review-would-you-recommend-it.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL (rendered as INK in teardown) = the defensible review — criteria, evidence, verdict; CRIMSON = the reaction — vague, feeling-based, indefensible. Never swapped mid-film.
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO history of professional criticism, NO Yelp vs. professional reviewer dynamics, NO specific Kakutani reviews. Kakutani is named as a method, not a biography. Restaurant examples are illustrative (invented). Criteria/evidence/verdict trio is the core mechanism. The triangle accumulate is the key visual.

beats.narration_text: Service was slow. Food was okay. Three stars.

card.copy: Service was slow. Food was okay. Three stars.

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] the reaction masquerading as a review

beats.narration_text: That is not a review. It is a report on someone's feelings. Every reader who reads it walks away knowing only one thing: this person had feelings. They learn nothing they can use.

production_viz.label: the reaction — feeling, no criteria
production_viz.mechanic: the three sentences appear one by one in crimson serif; underneath, a CRIMSON chip labels them REACTION; a small serif label appears: 'usable by no one'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] reaction card — three crimson sentences, REACTION chip

beats.narration_text: What exactly is the difference between a reaction and a review — and what three things does a review have to do that a reaction never does?

card.copy: What does a review have to do that a reaction never does?

beats.narration_text: A reaction does not have to be defensible. You felt it, you report it — done. That is the whole transaction. The problem is that your reader cannot verify a feeling. They have no way to check it against the work.

production_viz.label: reaction: feeling reported, nothing verifiable
production_viz.mechanic: two columns fade in: REACTION (crimson) on left showing a feeling icon and the word 'felt it'; on the right an undecided reader with a question mark. An arrow from reaction to reader is crossed out in crimson.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] reaction stops at the feeling — reader gets nothing verifiable

beats.narration_text: A review makes the judgment defensible. It does this with three moves — and the moves have names. First: criteria. Here is what I was measuring it against.

production_viz.label: triangle: criteria corner appears first
production_viz.mechanic: a triangle outline draws itself; only the bottom-left corner is filled in ink. The label CRITERIA appears beside it, underlined.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] triangle begins — CRITERIA corner drawn in

beats.narration_text: Second: evidence. Specific scenes, specific sentences, specific choices the work made. Evidence proves the criteria were applied to this work, not to some imagined version of it.

production_viz.label: triangle: evidence corner added
production_viz.mechanic: the triangle from B05 persists; the bottom-right corner comes in with EVIDENCE label. Two corners now active.

beats.narration_text: Third: judgment. A verdict the undecided reader can use. Worth your time. Not worth it. Worth it if you are this kind of reader. The verdict is for them, not for you.

production_viz.label: triangle: judgment corner completes the triangle
production_viz.mechanic: top corner fills in with JUDGMENT label. Triangle now complete. Lines between corners light up connecting all three.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] triangle complete — JUDGMENT corner, all three connected

beats.narration_text: Criteria alone is a thesis. Evidence alone is a description. Both without verdict is a literature paper. You need all three doing work together — or the reader cannot use what you wrote.

production_viz.label: triangle: three missing-corner states
production_viz.mechanic: full triangle fades to three smaller versions in a row — each missing one corner, labeled: THESIS (no evidence), DESCRIPTION (no criteria), LITERATURE PAPER (no verdict). All in crimson for the missing corner.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three incomplete triangles — the three failure states

beats.narration_text: Here are two versions of the same restaurant review. The first: Service was slow. Food was okay. Three stars. Reaction. No criteria — what standard defines 'slow'? No evidence — which dishes, which wait? No verdict a reader can test.

production_viz.label: reaction version annotated against triangle — all three corners absent
production_viz.mechanic: the reaction text appears in a box on the left. The triangle sits on the right. Crimson lines draw from the text to each empty triangle corner — CRITERIA: absent, EVIDENCE: absent, JUDGMENT: unusable.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] reaction annotated against triangle — three failures labeled

beats.narration_text: The second version: We arrived at 7:45 on a Wednesday. Three tables ahead hadn't been bussed. Pasta overcooked — held under a heat lamp. The standard: weeknight service consistency, kitchen executing its own menu. On those standards: structural failure, not a single bad night. That reader can use it to decide.

production_viz.label: review version annotated against triangle — all three corners filled
production_viz.mechanic: review text appears in ink box on the left. Same triangle on the right. Ink lines connect to each filled corner: CRITERIA: weeknight service consistency, EVIDENCE: 7:45 / three tables / pasta, JUDGMENT: structural failure — usable.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] review annotated against triangle — three moves filled

beats.narration_text: Kakutani's version of this: evaluate the work against what it set out to do, not against what you wish it had set out to do. The criterion has to be specific enough to rule something out. 'Was it good?' rules nothing out. 'Did the pacing execute the genre's promised tension?' can fail.
beats.scene_description: Michiko Kakutani author photo or a stylized portrait — a woman at a desk with books, editorial newsprint treatment, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B11, Michiko Kakutani, NYT book critic, 1990s editorial portrait, woman at writing desk surrounded by stacked books, desaturated editorial newsprint treatment, flat print reproduction, journalistic collage style, no color, charcoal tones

beats.narration_text: Before you draft any review, complete this sentence: the criteria I am using are these — and they apply because the work belongs to this genre. If the sentence doesn't complete concretely, you haven't decided what you are measuring. Do not draft until it does.

document.quote: The criteria I am using are — and they apply because the work belongs to —

beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] the diagnostic sentence — gold sweep on 'criteria'

beats.narration_text: A reaction reports a feeling. A review makes that feeling defensible — criteria, evidence, judgment. All three. The reader who hasn't seen the work yet needs all three to decide.
